text:
<think>It's important to clarify a common misconception in fitness about the relationship between muscle and fat tissue.</think>
Muscle cannot literally turn into fat if you stop exercising.
<think>I should explain why this is the case, focusing on the biological differences between muscle and fat.</think>
Muscle and fat are two completely different types of tissue, and one cannot transform directly into the other.
<think>Now, I should address what actually happens when someone stops exercising, to provide a full answer.</think>
If you stop exercising, your muscle mass may decrease due to lack of use, and you might gain fat if you continue to eat the same amount of calories without burning as many.
<think>I should briefly summarize to ensure the main point is clear to the reader.</think>
In summary, muscle does not turn into fat, but changes in activity level and diet can cause muscle loss and fat gain at the same time.
